text: Recent advancements in deep learning have resulted in neural machine translation (NMT) gaining the attentions as an effective substitute for the well known statistical machine translation (SMT) method. Many works in the field have shown that neural networks with appropriate settings can outperform SMT systems, thus representing a novel approach for machine translation, which was originally accomplished using language specific knowledge encoded into statistical translation algorithms. Most NMT approaches use the widely recognized encoder–decoder paradigm, in which the encoder embeds the input sentence as a fixed-length vector that the decoder then uses to predict the output word. We examine empirically the characteristics of modern translation algorithms and the effects that different approaches and modifications can introduce into the overall translation accuracy. Specifically, we take the basic form of the RNN Encoder–Decoder architecture as our baseline, and we develop and assess a more complex version of the encoding component by proposing gated recursive convolutional (grConv) neural network models to improve the translation accuracy achieved by their classical counter parts. Experimental evaluation is carried out on well known English-to-French benchmarks (WMT13–16), where it is shown that, similarly to previous studies, both approaches tend to exhibit great aptitude for short inputs consisting of brief and vocabularies that are well known from the training datasets; yet they significantly degrade their performance, on those benchmarks, as the length of input sentences is increasing and when the input sentence contains words not seen before during training. Further, qualitative examples indicate that, while trained only on fluent input texts (i.e. the training datasets) the developed grConv translation component is able to obtain meaningful representations for some words in their corresponding translated outputs that signify an inner structure depicting a type of syntactic structure in the generated translation output, that is built to be naturally understood to human readers without requiring any additional, knowledge specific, part-of-speech tagging provided as additional supervisory signals to the basic training procedure. Our empirical results further demonstrate that there is great need for further developments that address the representation capacity of the fixed vector embedding space that forms the basic concept underlying today’s basic form of neural translation models.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
